items.q: What defines a star as being on the 'main sequence'?
items.correct: It fuses hydrogen into helium in its core.
items.misconception: It is the largest stage of a star's life.
items.why: Students think main sequence = most spectacular. It's defined by the FUEL: hydrogen fusion. Stars spend most of their lives there because hydrogen is plentiful and fusion is stable.

items.q: What determines whether a star becomes a white dwarf or a neutron star?
items.correct: Its mass - low/medium mass becomes a white dwarf; massive stars produce neutron stars or black holes.
items.misconception: Its colour during its main sequence phase.
items.why: Students think colour determines end state. Mass is the key factor - low-mass stars (like the Sun) end as white dwarfs; high-mass stars supernova, leaving neutron stars or black holes depending on mass.

items.q: Why is Jupiter classified as a gas giant?
items.correct: Its bulk is mostly hydrogen and helium with no defined solid surface.
items.misconception: It is mostly air like Earth's atmosphere.
items.why: Students project Earth's atmosphere onto Jupiter. Jupiter has no rocky surface - it's gas transitioning to metallic hydrogen with depth. Very different from a thin atmosphere around a solid planet.

items.q: Supergiants sit in which area of the HR diagram?
items.why: It's Upper right. “Lower right” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An M dwarf is found in:
items.why: Correct answer: Lower right of MS. “Upper left of MS” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Approximate percentage of stars on the main sequence at any time?
items.why: Exact answer: 90. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
